root.narration: Top of the first inning, nobody on, nobody out. You're the leadoff hitter -- the first batter of the game! The opposing pitcher is warming up and looks nervous. They've been bouncing pitches in warm-ups. Your coach says, 'Be patient up there. Make the pitcher throw strikes.' You step into the batter's box. What's your approach?

choices.text: Be patient! Make the pitcher throw strikes. Take pitches and work the count.
Swing at the first pitch! Surprise the pitcher before they settle in!
Swing at everything! Put the ball in play no matter what!

outcome-patient-great.narration: You step in and take the first pitch -- it bounces in the dirt. Ball one! The second pitch is way high. Ball two! The pitcher is getting nervous. They throw the next one right down the middle because they HAVE to throw a strike. You're ready for it and drive it into right field for a clean single! You're on first base and the top of your lineup is coming up. Great at-bat!

outcome.headline: Leadoff Single! Patient At-Bat!
outcome.explanation: As the leadoff hitter, your NUMBER ONE job is to get on base. It doesn't matter how -- a hit, a walk, getting hit by a pitch. You just need to reach base so your teammates can drive you in. Being patient is the best approach because: (1) you see what pitches the pitcher throws, which helps your whole team learn their stuff, (2) you make the pitcher throw more pitches which tires them out, and (3) a nervous pitcher who's falling behind in the count HAS to throw a strike eventually, giving you a better pitch to hit. You took two balls, got ahead in the count, and crushed the pitcher's best strike!
outcome.whatToRemember: The leadoff hitter's job is to get on base. Be patient, make the pitcher throw strikes, and wait for YOUR pitch. A walk is just as good as a hit!

keyTerms.definition: The first batter in the lineup. Their main job is to get on base so the rest of the lineup can drive them in to score. Speed and patience are the most important traits.
Being patient and making the pitcher throw a lot of pitches. This helps you get a better pitch to hit AND tires the pitcher out for the rest of the game.
When there are more balls than strikes (like 2-0 or 3-1), the batter is 'ahead.' This means the pitcher HAS to throw something closer to the strike zone, giving the batter a better pitch to hit.

outcome-firstpitch-okay.narration: The pitcher's first pitch is a fastball and you swing at it. You hit it, but it's a weak ground ball to the shortstop. Easy out. One out, nobody on. Your at-bat lasted ONE pitch. The pitcher smiles -- they love quick outs because it saves their energy. Your teammates in the dugout didn't even have time to study the pitcher's stuff.

outcome.headline: Quick Out! One Pitch At-Bat!
outcome.explanation: Swinging at the first pitch isn't always bad -- if it's a perfect pitch right down the middle, sometimes you should attack it. But as the LEADOFF hitter, you have a special job. Your team hasn't seen this pitcher yet! By taking a few pitches, your teammates in the dugout get to see the pitcher's speed, movement, and location. This helps EVERYONE. Also, a one-pitch at-bat is a gift to the pitcher. They love it because it saves their arm. Making the pitcher work hard (throwing 5-6 pitches per batter) wears them down over the game.
outcome.whatToRemember: As the leadoff hitter, take a few pitches to help your team see the pitcher. One-pitch at-bats help the pitcher, not your team.

outcome-chase-bad.narration: You swing at the first pitch -- it bounces in the dirt and you miss. Strike one. You swing at the second pitch -- it's way over your head and you miss. Strike two. You swing at the third pitch -- it's in the other batter's box! You swing and miss. Strike three! Three pitches, three swings, zero contact, and you struck out in about 15 seconds. The pitcher didn't even have to throw a strike! Your coach sighs from the dugout.

outcome.headline: Three-Pitch Strikeout! No Discipline!
outcome.explanation: This is a pitcher's dream -- a batter who swings at EVERYTHING. None of those three pitches were in the strike zone, which means if you had just stood there, the count would be 3-0 and you'd be one pitch away from a free walk to first base! By swinging at everything, you let the pitcher get you out without ever throwing a strike. That's backwards! The batter is supposed to make the pitcher come to THEM by only swinging at strikes. If you swing at balls in the dirt and pitches over your head, the pitcher never has to throw anything hittable.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't swing at everything! If the pitch isn't in the strike zone, let it go. Make the pitcher throw strikes. You get four free balls before you walk -- use them!
